ui.keyboardHint: Finish each lesson quiz set to unlock the next lesson.
ui.heroTitle: Learn Go through small programs that surface the language model directly.
ui.heroBody: This track covers Go from syntax and zero values through interfaces, concurrency, the runtime, escape analysis, the memory model, garbage collection, testing, HTTP servers, and profiling. The goal is to learn both the language and the runtime model that explains why Go code behaves the way it does.
ui.lessonComplete: Lesson complete. The next lesson is now unlocked.
ui.incorrectReasonFallback: That choice does not match what the code or concept is doing.
ui.workspaceNote: This editor is for reading and tweaking the example. Use the external workspace for full execution and larger experiments.
ui.outputReady: Read the code, then verify your answer in the quiz.
ui.outputPlaceholder: Output is shown by the lesson metadata below.
ui.howItWorks1: Open a lesson and read the explanation next to a focused code sample.
ui.howItWorks2: Use the embedded editor to annotate or tweak the snippet while keeping the lesson context nearby.
ui.howItWorks3: Finish the quiz set, then continue in the external workspace when you want a larger sandbox.
ui.previewStatus: Move from values and control flow to scheduler behavior, GC, HTTP servers, tests, and profiling.
ui.homeTip: Edit examples here, then move to the official Go Playground when you want to run or expand them.

01_go_main.title: Program Entry
01_go_main.description:
A runnable Go program uses `package main` and a `main()` function.

```go
package main
func main() { fmt.Println("boot") }
```

q2.question: Why must an executable use `package main`?
options: The Go tool looks for a `main` package with a `main` function as the entry point.
Only `package main` can import the standard library.
It forces every variable to be global.
explanations: Correct. That is how the toolchain identifies an executable program.
Any package can import the standard library.
Package choice does not make variables global.
q2.answerReason: Go distinguishes executable entry packages from reusable libraries through `package main`.

q3.question: What happens if a package is not named `main`?
options: It cannot contain functions.
It becomes a test-only package automatically.
It can still compile as a library package, but not as the executable entry package.
explanations: Non-main packages can contain normal code.
Package name alone does not make it test-only.
Correct. Non-main packages are reusable packages rather than the binary entry package.
q3.answerReason: This is how Go keeps library code and executable entry points separate.

02_zero_values.title: Zero Values
02_zero_values.description:
Go gives variables a defined zero value.

```go
var count int
var ok bool
```

q2.question: What is the zero value of `int`?
options: `nil`
an undefined value
explanations: `nil` is for reference-like types.
Correct. Numeric zero values are defined.
Go avoids undefined local contents here.
q2.answerReason: Defined zero values shape many Go APIs.

q3.question: Why are zero values important in Go style?
options: They make many types usable immediately without explicit constructors.
They force all APIs to use global variables.
They eliminate the type system.
explanations: Correct. Go often aims for “the zero value is useful.”
Zero values do not imply global state.
They complement, not replace, the type system.
q3.answerReason: This is one reason Go APIs often feel lightweight.

03_functions_returns.title: Functions and Multiple Returns
03_functions_returns.description: Go functions can return more than one value, which makes patterns like `(value, err)` natural.

q2.question: Why are multiple return values useful?
options: They remove the need for control flow.
They define multiple packages at once.
They let a function return both a result and status such as an error.
explanations: Control flow is still needed.
Packages are unrelated.
Correct. This is a major reason Go error handling looks the way it does.
q2.answerReason: The language makes status-return patterns first-class.

q3.question: What does `left, right := swap(3, 7)` do?
options: It declares only `left`.
It declares two variables and binds returned values positionally.
It copies the function body into `main`.
explanations: Both variables are declared together.
Correct. Return positions map to assignment positions.
Calls do not work by source substitution.
q3.answerReason: Go binds multiple returned values by position.

04_pointers.title: Pointers and Mutation
04_pointers.description: Go has pointers, but not general pointer arithmetic in safe code. Pointers mostly model shared access and mutation.

q2.question: Why pass `*int` here?
options: The function needs access to the caller-owned value so it can mutate it.
Go forbids passing integers by value.
Only pointers can be printed.
explanations: Correct. A pointer lets the function change the original variable.
Value parameters are normal in Go.
Printing is unrelated.
q2.answerReason: Pointers let a function modify the caller-owned value rather than a copy.

q3.question: What is a key constraint on pointers in Go compared with C?
options: Pointers cannot point to values.
Pointers always live on the stack.
Go does not expose arbitrary pointer arithmetic in safe code.
explanations: They can point to values normally.
Escape analysis may move values; stack-only is false.
Correct. Go keeps pointer usage narrower than C.
q3.answerReason: Go uses pointers for sharing access, not for arbitrary low-level arithmetic in safe code.

05_arrays_slices.title: Arrays and Slices
05_arrays_slices.description: Slices are descriptors over underlying array storage. That is why mutating one slice can affect another view.

q2.question: Why does changing `part[0]` affect `nums[0]`?
options: Slices synchronize indexes automatically.
The slice still points to the same backing array.
Slicing deep-copies the array.
explanations: There is no automatic sync rule.
Correct. A slice is metadata over shared storage.
Basic slicing does not deep-copy the backing array.
q2.answerReason: Thinking “slice = descriptor” explains aliasing and append behavior.

q3.question: What is the main conceptual difference between an array and a slice?
options: An array owns fixed-size storage; a slice describes a region of underlying storage.
Arrays only hold numbers.
Slices are always immutable.
explanations: Correct. Arrays are concrete fixed-size values; slices are views.
Both can hold many element types.
Slices are mutable when their elements are mutable.
q3.answerReason: This distinction drives length, capacity, and aliasing semantics.

06_maps.title: Maps and Presence
06_maps.description: Map lookup often returns a value together with a boolean flag. The flag tells you whether the key was present at all.

q2.question: Why is the `ok` result useful?
options: It distinguishes a missing key from a present key whose value is the zero value.
It skips hashing.
It orders the map.
explanations: Correct. Presence and value are separate facts.
Hashing still happens.
Maps remain unordered.
q2.answerReason: Without `ok`, `0` could mean “absent” or “present with zero value.”

q3.question: Why can the value result alone be ambiguous?
options: Because Go randomizes missing results.
Because the zero value may also be a legitimate stored value.
Because maps always return strings internally.
explanations: Go does not randomize missing values.
Correct. Zero can be a valid stored result too.
Map results keep their declared type.
q3.answerReason: The extra boolean exists because zero and absent are different states.

07_structs_methods.title: Structs and Methods
07_structs_methods.description: Go attaches methods to receiver types rather than using classes. Pointer receivers are common when mutation matters.

q2.question: Why is the receiver a pointer here?
options: The method needs to mutate the original struct value.
Go requires all receivers to be pointers.
Only pointer receivers can call `fmt.Println`.
explanations: Correct. Mutation should affect the caller-owned value.
Value receivers also exist.
Printing is unrelated.
q2.answerReason: Receiver choice decides whether a method sees the same value or a copy.

q3.question: What would likely happen with a value receiver instead?
options: The program would stop compiling.
The call would update both the original and an automatic backup.
The method would increment a copy, leaving the caller-owned struct unchanged.
explanations: Value receivers are legal.
There is no hidden backup rule.
Correct. Value receivers work on a copy.
q3.answerReason: Methods on value receivers cannot mutate the caller-owned struct directly.

08_interfaces.title: Interfaces and Implicit Satisfaction
08_interfaces.description: Go interfaces describe behavior, and types satisfy them implicitly by method set.

q2.question: How does `Job` satisfy `Runner`?
options: It must declare `implements Runner`.
It has the required method, so it satisfies the interface implicitly.
Interfaces are checked only through reflection.
explanations: Go has no `implements` keyword.
Correct. Satisfaction is structural and compile-time checked.
This is not deferred to reflection.
q2.answerReason: Interfaces in Go are about behavior a value has, not declared inheritance.

q3.question: Why is implicit satisfaction useful?
options: Consumers can define small interfaces around needed behavior without modifying the concrete type.
It forces all packages to share one interface registry.
It prevents compile-time verification.
explanations: Correct. This supports decoupled, consumer-owned abstractions.
Go’s model reduces that coupling.
Compile-time verification still happens.
q3.answerReason: Small consumer-owned interfaces are a major Go design strength.

09_errors.title: Errors Are Values
09_errors.description: Go treats errors as ordinary returned values rather than exceptions for ordinary failure paths.

q2.question: Why does Go often return `error` explicitly?
options: To avoid all control flow.
Because Go has no returns.
Because failure is treated as data the caller must inspect explicitly.
explanations: Control flow still exists.
Go obviously has returns.
Correct. The caller decides how to handle failure.
q2.answerReason: Go prefers explicit failure handling over hidden control transfer for ordinary errors.

q3.question: What does “errors are values” emphasize?
options: Errors should always be ignored after logging.
An error participates in normal program logic like any other returned value.
Only numeric types can represent errors.
explanations: Ignoring errors is the opposite of the lesson.
Correct. Errors are passed, checked, wrapped, and returned explicitly.
Errors are interface values, not numeric-only values.
q3.answerReason: This design encourages visible, deliberate failure handling.

10_defer_panic_recover.title: Defer, Panic, and Recover
10_defer_panic_recover.description: `defer` schedules cleanup for function exit. `panic` is for unrecoverable states, not ordinary business failures.

q2.question: What is the main use of `defer`?
options: To ensure cleanup-like work runs when the current function returns.
To launch goroutines automatically.
To convert every error into a panic.
explanations: Correct. `defer` is common for unlock/close/cleanup patterns.
`defer` does not create goroutines.
Errors and panics are different tools.
q2.answerReason: `defer` keeps cleanup close to acquisition while preserving return-order behavior.

q3.question: Why is `panic` usually not the default error-handling mechanism?
options: Because `panic` is faster.
Because panics are required for all I/O failures.
Because ordinary failures are expected control-flow cases that callers should handle explicitly.
explanations: Speed is not the reason.
Routine I/O errors are normally returned, not panicked.
Correct. `panic` is for exceptional or unrecoverable states.
q3.answerReason: Using panic for routine failures makes control flow harder to reason about.

11_goroutines.title: Goroutines and Process Lifetime
11_goroutines.description: A goroutine is a lightweight concurrent execution managed by the runtime, but it still lives inside one process that may exit first.

q2.question: Why is `WaitGroup` used here?
options: To keep `main` alive until the goroutine finishes.
To pin the goroutine to one CPU core.
To share locals by reference automatically.
explanations: Correct. Without synchronization, the program could exit too early.
`WaitGroup` does not pin goroutines.
Variable capture is unrelated.
q2.answerReason: Concurrency starts work; synchronization decides whether the process waits for it.

q3.question: What is the risk if `main` returns immediately after launching a goroutine?
options: The goroutine becomes a saved daemon.
The process may exit before the goroutine finishes.
The runtime converts the goroutine into a channel.
explanations: Goroutines do not survive process exit.
Correct. Program lifetime still matters.
There is no such conversion.
q3.answerReason: A goroutine is lightweight, but it still lives inside a process that can end first.

12_channels_select.title: Channels and Select
12_channels_select.description: Channels coordinate communication between goroutines. `select` waits on multiple communication cases.

q2.question: What is the main role of a channel?
options: To sort messages.
To guarantee all goroutines run sequentially.
To coordinate communication and handoff between goroutines.
explanations: Channels do not sort values.
They do not make the whole program sequential.
Correct. Communication is the main abstraction.
q2.answerReason: Channels make ownership transfer and coordination explicit.

q3.question: Why is `select` useful?
options: It lets one goroutine react to whichever channel operation becomes ready first.
It replaces all loops.
It forces every case to run in order.
explanations: Correct. `select` multiplexes communication cases.
Loops are still useful.
Ready case selection is not fixed sequential execution.
q3.answerReason: `select` is the communication-oriented control-flow primitive for multiple possible events.

13_mutex_waitgroup.title: Mutexes and Shared State
13_mutex_waitgroup.description: Channels are not the only concurrency tool. Sometimes you have shared mutable state and need explicit locking.

q2.question: What problem does a mutex solve?
options: It makes every goroutine faster automatically.
It prevents conflicting concurrent access to shared mutable state.
It creates new goroutines on lock acquisition.
explanations: A mutex is about correctness, not automatic speed.
Correct. Mutual exclusion protects critical sections.
Locks do not spawn goroutines.
q2.answerReason: A mutex serializes access where simultaneous mutation would be unsafe.

q3.question: What is the tradeoff when you add a mutex?
options: You eliminate the need to reason about concurrency entirely.
You guarantee maximum parallelism.
You gain correctness for shared updates, but the critical section may reduce concurrency.
explanations: You still must reason about what is shared.
Serialization can reduce, not maximize, parallelism.
Correct. Locks trade some concurrency freedom for safety.
q3.answerReason: Locking is a correctness tool first; performance depends on contention and design.

14_context_timeout.title: Context Cancellation and Deadlines
14_context_timeout.description: A `context.Context` carries cancellation signals, deadlines, and small amounts of request-scoped metadata through call chains.

q2.question: Why pass `context.Context` through a request path?
options: It lets downstream work observe cancellation and deadlines consistently.
It replaces ordinary business parameters.
It forces synchronous execution.
explanations: Correct. It propagates lifecycle signals across layers.
Normal inputs are usually clearer as explicit parameters.
Context does not force synchronous behavior.
q2.answerReason: Cancellation only helps when every relevant layer can see the same signal.

q3.question: What kind of data should usually not be hidden in `context.Context`?
options: Cancellation and deadline information.
Ordinary business parameters that should be explicit arguments.
Request-scoped metadata for cross-cutting middleware concerns.
explanations: Those are core context responsibilities.
Correct. Normal inputs should remain visible in the function signature.
Some scoped metadata can live in context carefully.
q3.answerReason: Context is for lifecycle and cross-cutting signals, not for hiding routine domain data.

15_escape_analysis.title: Escape Analysis
15_escape_analysis.description: Go decides whether a value can stay on the stack or must move to the heap. If a value escapes the frame, stack-only lifetime is not enough.

q2.question: Why can returning `&x` be safe in Go?
options: Because pointers to locals are always invalid but tolerated.
Because escape analysis can move `x` to the heap when the value must outlive the function frame.
Because Go copies the whole stack forever.
explanations: Go does not knowingly expose invalid pointers in safe code.
Correct. The compiler/runtime preserves the needed lifetime.
Whole stack frames are not copied forever.
q2.answerReason: Escape analysis helps decide when stack allocation is insufficient for the required lifetime.

q3.question: What is the core question escape analysis answers?
options: Whether the program imports too many packages.
Whether the pointer type implements an interface.
Whether a value must outlive the current stack frame.
explanations: Imports are unrelated.
Interfaces are unrelated.
Correct. Lifetime beyond the frame is the key question.
q3.answerReason: Allocation placement follows lifetime needs, not just syntax.

16_gc_basic.title: Garbage Collection and Reachability
16_gc_basic.description: Go uses garbage collection to reclaim heap objects that are no longer reachable from live roots.

q2.question: What keeps an object alive for the garbage collector?
options: It is still reachable from live roots such as stacks, globals, or runtime-managed references.
It has existed for fewer than ten seconds.
It was allocated with a pointer type.
explanations: Correct. Reachability is the key property.
Age alone is not the rule.
Pointer type syntax alone does not guarantee liveness.
q2.answerReason: GC is about reachability from live roots, not manual delete calls.

q3.question: Why should a Go developer still care how GC works?
options: Because every Go program must call `free` manually.
Because allocation rate, object lifetime, and pointer-rich structures affect runtime and latency behavior.
Because GC eliminates all memory performance tradeoffs.
explanations: Go does not require manual `free`.
Correct. GC removes one class of bugs, but runtime cost still depends on allocation behavior.
GC does not erase performance tradeoffs.
q3.answerReason: Knowing the GC model helps explain heap pressure, latency, and throughput tradeoffs.

17_gc_phases.title: GC Phases and Write Barriers
17_gc_phases.description: Modern tracing collectors coordinate marking and sweeping while the program keeps running. Write barriers help maintain correctness during concurrent marking.

q2.question: Why does a concurrent GC need a write barrier?
options: Because the program can keep mutating references while the collector is tracing object graph reachability.
Because the barrier encrypts pointers.
Because write barriers delete all short-lived objects instantly.
explanations: Correct. Concurrent mutation would otherwise break the collector’s view of reachability.
Encryption is unrelated.
Write barriers are about correctness, not instant deletion.
q2.answerReason: If the heap changes while the collector is tracing it, the collector needs help preserving a correct reachability picture.

q3.question: What is the high-level goal of Go’s modern GC design?
options: To stop the world for the entire lifetime of the program.
To avoid all runtime overhead from memory management.
To balance automatic memory safety with acceptable pause times and throughput.
explanations: A collector that stopped the world forever would be useless.
Automatic memory management still has cost.
Correct. GC design is about balancing correctness, latency, and throughput.
q3.answerReason: Runtime engineering is about tradeoffs, not free elimination of all costs.

18_memory_model.title: The Go Memory Model
18_memory_model.description: Concurrent correctness is not only about syntax. The memory model defines when writes from one goroutine are guaranteed to become visible to another.

q2.question: Why does the memory model matter in concurrent Go code?
options: Because it changes package import rules.
Because visibility and ordering between goroutines need explicit synchronization guarantees.
Because it makes integers larger at runtime.
explanations: Imports are unrelated.
Correct. Concurrency correctness depends on ordering and visibility guarantees.
Integer size is unrelated.
q2.answerReason: Without synchronization, even “simple” shared-state reasoning can be wrong.

q3.question: What usually creates a happens-before relationship in Go?
options: Synchronization operations such as channel communication, mutex unlock/lock ordering, or other documented primitives.
Two goroutines printing to stdout.
Using short variable declarations.
explanations: Correct. Happens-before is created by specific synchronization semantics.
Printing is not a general memory-ordering primitive.
Variable declaration syntax does not synchronize goroutines.
q3.answerReason: The memory model is about when one event’s effects are guaranteed visible to another.

19_generics.title: Generics and Type Parameters
19_generics.description: Go generics let one function or type work across multiple concrete types while preserving static type checking.

q2.question: Why are generics useful?
options: They remove the need for types.
They make every type interchangeable at runtime.
They let reusable algorithms stay type-safe across multiple concrete types.
explanations: Types still matter strongly.
Generic code is still statically checked.
Correct. Generics reduce duplication without giving up static type checking.
q2.answerReason: Generics are about reusable structure without dropping into `interface{}` everywhere.

q3.question: What does `T any` mean here?
options: `T` must be an integer.
`T` is a type parameter with no extra constraint beyond being any type.
`T` is a runtime variable.
explanations: No integer-only restriction is present.
Correct. `any` is the broadest constraint.
Type parameters are compile-time type-level entities, not runtime variables.
q3.answerReason: Constraints describe what operations generic code may assume about type arguments.

20_testing_table_driven.title: Table-Driven Tests
20_testing_table_driven.description: Go tests often use tables of input and expected output so one test structure can exercise many cases.

q2.question: Why are table-driven tests common in Go?
options: They make it easy to run one test shape across many cases with clear input/output expectations.
They replace the need for assertions.
They only work for string functions.
explanations: Correct. The style keeps tests compact and scalable.
Assertions or checks still matter.
They work across many kinds of logic.
q2.answerReason: The pattern keeps test structure uniform while expanding case coverage.

q3.question: What is a major advantage of table-driven tests during maintenance?
options: They make production code faster automatically.
They prevent all bugs.
New edge cases can often be added by extending test data rather than rewriting the test structure.
explanations: Tests do not directly optimize production speed.
No test style prevents all bugs.
Correct. Extending the case table is often simple and readable.
q3.answerReason: Good test structure lowers the cost of broadening coverage over time.

21_benchmarks_pprof.title: Benchmarks and Profiling
21_benchmarks_pprof.description: Go includes built-in support for benchmarks and tooling such as `pprof`. Performance work should be measured, not guessed.

q2.question: Why use benchmarks before performance claims?
options: Because benchmarks change the compiler.
Because optimization work should be grounded in measured behavior rather than intuition alone.
Because benchmarks automatically fix slow code.
explanations: Benchmarks do not change language semantics.
Correct. Performance work needs measurement.
Benchmarks report; they do not fix code themselves.
q2.answerReason: Systems performance work should start with evidence, not hunches.

q3.question: What does a profiler like `pprof` help you find?
options: Where time or allocation is actually going in a real execution profile.
How to rename packages.
How to turn all goroutines into threads.
explanations: Correct. Profiling identifies hot paths and allocation patterns.
Renaming packages is unrelated.
Profilers do not change the concurrency model.
q3.answerReason: Optimization gets much easier once you know where the program is actually spending resources.

22_http_handler.title: HTTP Handlers
22_http_handler.description: A handler is an entry point that receives a request and writes a response. This is the transport boundary of many Go services.

q2.question: What is a handler’s job conceptually?
options: To replace the scheduler.
To implement garbage collection.
To receive a request, run application logic, and write a response.
explanations: Scheduling is unrelated.
GC is unrelated.
Correct. A handler is the boundary between HTTP traffic and application logic.
q2.answerReason: Handlers are transport-layer entry points for incoming requests.

q3.question: Why is request flow design important even in a simple handler?
options: Because clear boundaries between parsing, business logic, and response writing keep services maintainable.
Because handlers should contain every database query directly.
Because request methods are ignored by `net/http`.
explanations: Correct. Good boundaries matter early, not only in large services.
Putting everything in handlers creates tight coupling.
HTTP methods absolutely matter.
q3.answerReason: Even small services benefit when transport concerns and domain logic stay distinct.

23_interfaces_in_practice.title: Interfaces in Practice
23_interfaces_in_practice.description: In idiomatic Go, small interfaces are often defined by the consumer, not the concrete implementation package.

q2.question: Why are small consumer-owned interfaces often preferable?
options: They reduce coupling and describe only the behavior a caller actually needs.
They force every implementation to import the consumer package.
They remove compile-time checking.
explanations: Correct. Narrow interfaces keep dependencies lighter.
Implementations do not need to import the consumer package just to satisfy the interface.
Compile-time checking remains.
q2.answerReason: Designing interfaces around use cases rather than concrete types keeps packages more flexible.

q3.question: What is a common smell in Go interface design?
options: An interface with one or two methods focused on a use case.
An interface defined by the consumer.
A huge interface that mirrors an implementation instead of the caller’s minimal need.
explanations: Small focused interfaces are often good.
Consumer-defined interfaces are common and useful.
Correct. Large implementation-shaped interfaces often create unnecessary coupling.
q3.answerReason: Good interfaces are shaped by the abstraction boundary the caller needs, not by the full concrete type surface.

24_scheduler_gmp.title: Scheduler: G, M, and P
24_scheduler_gmp.description: The Go runtime scheduler maps many goroutines onto a smaller set of OS threads using its own runtime model.

q2.question: Why does Go have its own goroutine scheduler instead of mapping every goroutine 1:1 to an OS thread?
options: Because OS threads cannot run Go code.
Because lightweight goroutines are intended to be cheaper and more numerous than OS threads.
Because the runtime forbids parallel execution.
explanations: Go code can run on OS threads normally.
Correct. The runtime scheduler exists to support many lightweight goroutines efficiently.
The runtime supports parallel execution.
q2.answerReason: The point is to let concurrency structure stay cheap and abundant.

q3.question: Why should a Go developer care about scheduler behavior?
options: Because blocking, wakeups, and goroutine structure can affect throughput and latency in real systems.
Because scheduler knowledge replaces the language syntax.
Because the scheduler removes all need for synchronization.
explanations: Correct. Runtime scheduling affects how concurrent work actually behaves.
Syntax is unrelated.
Synchronization is still needed.
q3.answerReason: The runtime model explains why some concurrency patterns scale well and others do not.

25_cgo_unsafe_boundary.title: Unsafe and cgo Boundaries
25_cgo_unsafe_boundary.description: Most Go code stays in safe, managed territory. `unsafe` and cgo exist for special cases, but they cross important abstraction boundaries.

q2.question: Why are `unsafe` and cgo considered boundaries rather than normal defaults?
options: Because they only work on Tuesdays.
Because they remove the Go type system completely at compile time.
Because they trade some safety or runtime assumptions for low-level interop and control.
explanations: There is no day-of-week rule.
They do not erase the whole compiler; they introduce targeted escape hatches.
Correct. These are deliberate boundary-crossing tools.
q2.answerReason: The power of these tools comes from stepping outside some of Go’s usual guarantees.

q3.question: What is the main engineering risk when using such boundaries?
options: They make source files shorter.
They bypass some of the guarantees that normally keep ordinary Go code simple and safe.
They remove all performance costs.
explanations: File length is irrelevant.
Correct. Escape hatches are risky because they weaken normal guarantees.
These tools do not magically remove all costs.
q3.answerReason: Use boundary-crossing tools deliberately and locally, not as the default architecture.
